nativeRange.habitat: Found in aggregations a few meters above the edges of current-swept drop-offs on outer reef slopes; typically collected from 20-60 m depth, with the broader recorded range extending from near the surface to roughly 180 m.

decorPreferences: generous live rock with caves, overhangs and slopes
open mid-water swimming space above the rockwork
subdued or indirect lighting, especially during acclimation
tightly sealed lid to prevent jumping

diet.notes: Wild diet is zooplankton picked from the water column above the reef. In aquaria, they generally accept frozen mysis shrimp and enriched frozen brine shrimp, and over time may take high-quality flake or pellet foods offered in small amounts. Multiple small feedings per day (3-5) are commonly advised, and an attached refugium cultivating copepods and amphipods is widely considered helpful for long-term condition.

avoidWith: additional Pseudanthias pleurotaenia males in the same system
very fast feeders that outcompete it at meal times

summary: A larger Indo-Pacific anthias generally kept singly or as a male-with-females harem; needs a long, well-fed system with open swimming space and several small feedings per day.
careNotes: The Squareback Anthias is a larger Pseudanthias (commonly around 18-20 cm at full size) generally considered intermediate in care. It is a protogynous (female-first) sequential hermaphrodite that naturally forms harems of one dominant male with several females; if the male is lost, the largest female has been reported to transition to male. Like other anthias it is a high-metabolism planktivore that tends to decline rapidly without 3-5 small feedings per day, and an attached refugium is commonly recommended. Specimens are generally collected from deep, low-light reef slopes and have been reported to acclimate poorly to bright reef lighting at first - subdued or indirect lighting during the first weeks is widely advised, and even long-term keepers tend to use dimmer blues to help preserve the male's pink coloration. Anthias are well-known jumpers, so a tightly sealed lid is generally recommended. Tankmate planning: keep one male per system - multiple males have been reported to fight, sometimes lethally - and pair him with two or more females if a harem is desired. Cross-source sizing: FishBase records a max length of 20 cm TL and LiveAquaria lists adults to about 7.75 inches (~20 cm), so the 13-20 cm range here brackets both. Tank-size note: LiveAquaria advises about 100 US gallons (~379 L) for a single fish and 180 US gallons (~681 L) for a group; the 400 L minimum here rounds the single-fish figure up to a standard size and assumes a 150 cm footprint for the species' active mid-water swimming - a 700 L+ system is more appropriate for a full harem.
breedingNotes: Not routinely bred in home aquaria. The species is a protogynous broadcast spawner; closely related Pseudanthias spp. have been reported to spawn at dusk and occasionally hybridize in the wild.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Anthiadidae, Perciformes), distribution (Indonesia to Samoa, Ryukyus to New Caledonia), depth range (0-180 m, usually 0-25 m), max length (20.0 cm TL), and preferred temperature (22-27 C). LiveAquaria's 72-78 F (~22.2-25.6 C) and FishBase's 22-27 C were averaged to a 22-26 C operating range.

additional.name: BlueZoo Aquatics
additional.notes: Tertiary confirmation of adult size (~8 in / 20 cm), 100 gal minimum, semi-aggressive temperament, reef-safe status, planktivore diet with 4+ daily feedings, and one-male-per-system harem rule.
